title: è¶ٹهچ— ???? #1
problem_md:
The sequence of integers $\{a_n\}$, $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$, is defined by:
$a_0 = 1$ and $a_n = a_{n-1} + a_{\lceil n/3 \rceil}$ for every $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$.
Prove that for every prime number $p \le 13$, there exists an infinite number of natural numbers $k$ such that $a_k$ is divisible by $p$.
([x] denotes the integral part of x).

title: è¶ٹهچ— ???? #2

Let $S$ be the point of intersection of the tangents at $P$ and at $Q$ of the circumcircle of triangle $APQ$ and let $H$ be the reflection of $B$ in the line $PQ$. title: è¶ٹهچ— ???? #3
problem_md: Let be given a positive integer $n$ and two coprime integers $a, b$ greater than $1$. Let $p, q$ be two odd divisors greater than $1$ of $a^{6n} + b^{6n}$. Find the remainder of the division of $p^{6n} + q^{6n}$ by $6 \cdot (12)^n$.
solution_md:
The answer follows easily from the following remarks:

a) If $a, b$ are coprime integers greater than $1$ and $p$ is an odd prime divisor of $a^{6n} + b^{6n}$ then $p \equiv 1 \pmod{2^{n+1}}$.

b) If $x \equiv 1 \pmod{c^k}$ then $x^{c^m} \equiv 1 \pmod{2^{m+k}}$.

title: è¶ٹهچ— ???? #5
problem_md:
For every couple of real numbers $(a, b)$, consider the sequence of numbers $\{x_n\}$, $n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$, defined by:
$$
x_0 = a \quad \text{and} \quad x_{n+1} = x_n + b \sin x_n \quad \text{for every } n = 0, 1, 2, \dots
$$
Prove that:
1) for every real number $a$, the sequence $\{x_n\}$ corresponding to $(a, b)$ has a finite limit when $n$ tends to infinity. Find this limit.
2) for every number $b > 2$, there exists a real number $a$ such that the sequence $\{x_n\}$ corresponding to $(a, b)$ does not have a finite limit when $n$ tends to infinity.
solution_md:
1).
+ For $a = k\pi$ ($k \in \mathbb{Z}$), we have $x_n = k\pi \quad \forall n \in \mathbb{N}$, therefore $\lim_{n \to \infty} x_n = k\pi$.

+ For $a \neq k\pi$ ($k \in \mathbb{Z}$), consider the function $f(x) = x + \sin x$ defined on $\mathbb{R}$. We have $f'(x) = 1 + \cos x \ge 0 \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}$. It follows that:

i) If $a \in (2k\pi; (2k+1)\pi)$ the sequence $\{x_n\}$ is increasing and bounded above by $(2k+1)\pi$, and it is easy to see that $\lim_{n \to \infty} x_n = (2k+1)\pi$.

ii) If $a \in ((2k-1)\pi; 2k\pi)$ the sequence $\{x_n\}$ is decreasing and bounded below by $(2k-1)\pi$, and it is easy to see that $\lim_{n \to \infty} x_n = (2k-1)\pi$.

Thus, for every $a \in \mathbb{R}$, the sequence $\{x_n\}$ corresponding to $(a, 1)$ is convergent and $\lim_{n \to \infty} x_n = (2[a/2\pi]+\operatorname{sign}({a/2\pi}))\pi$.

2).
For $b > 2$, it is easy to show that there exists $a_0 \in (0; \pi)$ such that $2a_0 = b \cdot \sin a_0$ and the sequence $\{x_n\}$ corresponding to $(a = \pi - a_0, b)$ is a periodic sequence of period 2.

title: è¶ٹهچ— ???? #6
problem_md:
Let $x$, $y$, $z$ be positive real numbers satisfying the following conditions:
$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \leq z < \frac{1}{2} \min \{x\sqrt{2},\ y\sqrt{3}\}, \\
 x + z\sqrt{3} \geq \sqrt{6}, \\
 y\sqrt{3} + z\sqrt{10} \geq 2\sqrt{5}.
$$
Find the greatest value of the expression:
$$
P(x, y, z) = \frac{1}{x^2} + \frac{2}{y^2} + \frac{3}{z^2}
$$
solution_md:
By denoting $\frac{1}{x\sqrt{2}} = a$, $\frac{1}{y\sqrt{3}} = b$, $\frac{1}{2z} = c$, the problem becomes:
Find the greatest value of the expression
$$
Q(a, b, c) = 2a^2 + 6b^2 + 12c^2
$$
where $a$, $b$, $c$ are positive numbers satisfying the conditions:
$$
\max\{a, b\} < c \leq \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \quad (1)
$$
$$
c\sqrt{2} + a\sqrt{3} \geq 2\sqrt{6} \text{ ac.} \quad (2)
$$
$$
c\sqrt{2} + b\sqrt{5} \geq 2\sqrt{10} \text{ bc.} \quad (3)
$$
We have from (2):
$$
\frac{\sqrt{2}}{a} + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{c} \geq 2\sqrt{6} \Rightarrow \frac{2}{a^2} + \frac{3}{c^2} \geq 12 \Rightarrow \frac{1}{6}a^2\left(\frac{2}{a^2} + \frac{3}{c^2}\right) \geq 2a^2,
$$
$$
\text{hence } a^2 + c^2 = 2a^2 + c^2 - a^2 \leq \frac{1}{6}a^2\left(\frac{2}{a^2} + \frac{3}{c^2}\right) + c^2\left(1 - \frac{a^2}{c^2}\right) \leq \frac{1}{6}a^2\left(\frac{2}{a^2} + \frac{3}{c^2}\right) + \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - \frac{a^2}{c^2}\right) = \frac{5}{6}.
$$
Analogously, from (1) and (3) we have: $b^2 + c^2 \leq \frac{7}{10}$.
Thus,
$$
Q(a, b, c) = 2(a^2 + c^2) + 6(b^2 + c^2) + 4c^2 \leq \frac{118}{15}.
$$
It is easy to verify that $Q(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}) = \frac{118}{15}$ and the values $a = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$, $b = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}$, $c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ satisfy the conditions (1) - (2) - (3).
$$
\text{Conclusion: } \max P(x, y, z) = \max Q(a, b, c) = \frac{118}{15}.
$$

title: è¶ٹهچ— ???? #8
problem_md:
Consider the function $g(x) = \frac{2x}{1+x^2}$. Find all function $f(x)$ defined and continuous on the interval $(-1; 1)$ which satisfy the condition:
$$
(1 - x^2)f(g(x)) = (1 + x^2)^2 f(x)
$$
for every $x \in (-1; 1)$.
solution_md:
The function $f(x)$ satisfies the demanded conditions when and only when the function $\varphi(x) = (1-x^2)f(x)$ is defined and continuous on $(-1;1)$ and satisfies the condition
$$
\varphi(g(x)) = \varphi(x) \quad \forall x \in (-1; 1). \quad (1)
$$
Consider the function $h(x)$ defined on $(0; +\infty)$ by $h(x) = \varphi\left(\frac{1-x}{1+x}\right)$ for all $x \in (0; +\infty)$. Then $\varphi(x)$ is continuous on $(-1;1)$ and satisfies (1) when and only when the function $h(x)$ is continuous on $(0; +\infty)$ and satisfies the condition
$$
h(x^2) = h(x) \quad \forall x \in (0; +\infty). \quad (2)
$$
It is easy to see that from (2), $h(x) = h(\sqrt{x})$ for all $x \in (0; +\infty)$ and for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$; and hence, from the continuity of $h$, $h(x) = h(1)$ for all $x \in (0; +\infty)$. Thus, $\varphi(x) = \text{const}$ for all $x \in (-1;1)$ and finally, $f(x) = \frac{a}{1-x^2}$ ($a \in \mathbb{R}$). One can verify that these functions $f(x)$ satisfy the demanded conditions.

title: è¶ٹهچ— ???? #9
problem_md: Let be given an integer $n > 1$ and an orthonormal system of coordinates $Oxyz$ in space. Let $T$ be the set of all points $P(x, y, z)$, the coordinates $x, y, z$ of which are integers satisfying the conditions $1 \le x, y, z \le n$. Colour some points in $T$ so that if the point $A(x_0, y_0, z_0)$ had been coloured then every point $B(x_1, y_1, z_1)$ with $x_1 \le x_0, y_1 \le y_0, z_1 \le z_0$ ($B \ne A$) could not be coloured. How many points at most can be coloured?

title: è¶ٹهچ— ???? #10
problem_md:
Let be given an integer $n \ge 1$. Consider a permutation $(a_1, a_2, ..., a_{2n})$ of the first $2n$ positive integers such that the numbers $|a_{i+1} - a_i|$ $(i = 1, 2, ..., 2n-1)$ are distinct each from others.
Prove that $a_1 - a_{2n} = n$ if and only if $1 \le a_{2k} \le n$ for every $k = 1, 2, ..., n$.
solution_md:
a) Sufficient condition. As $1 \le a_{2k} \le n$ ($\forall k = 1, 2, \dots, n$), we have:
$$
T = \sum_{i=1}^{2n-1} |a_{i+1} - a_i| = 2(a_1 + a_3 + \dots + a_{2n-1}) - 2(a_2 + a_4 + \dots + a_{2n}) + a_{2n} - a_1 = 2n^2 + a_{2n} - a_1.
$$
On the other hand, since $1 \le |a_{i+1} - a_i| \le 2n - 1$ $\forall i = 1, 2, \dots, 2n - 1$, and since the numbers $|a_{i+1} - a_i|$ are distinct, it is easy to see that $T = 2n^2 - n$. It follows that $a_{2n} - a_1 = n$.

b). Necessary condition. We have:
$$
T_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{2n-1} |a_{i+1} - a_i| + a_1 - a_{2n} = 2n^2. \quad (1)
$$
But $T_0$ can be written in the form $T_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{2n} \delta_i a_i$, where $\delta_i \in \{-2, 0, 2\}$ ($i=1, 2, \dots, 2n$) and it is easy to see that:
i) $\delta_1 + \delta_2 + \dots + \delta_{2n} = 0$, and
ii) if we erase all zeros in the sequence $\delta_1, \delta_2, \dots, \delta_{2n}$ and retain all numbers distinct from 0, we obtain a sequence where the numbers -2 and 2 stand alternately.
From i) we can prove that
$$
T_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{2n} \delta_i a_i = \sum_{i=1}^{2n} \delta_i (a_i - n) \le 2n^2, \quad \text{and the equality occurs when and only when}
$$
$$
\delta_i = 2 \text{ for all } i \text{ such that } a_i > n \text{ and } \delta_i = -2 \text{ for all } i \text{ such that } a_i \le n. \quad (2)
$$
As $a_1 - a_{2n} = n$, $a_{2n} \le n$.
Therefor, from (1), (2) and ii), we have: $1 \le a_{2k} \le n$ $\forall k = 1, 2, \dots, n$.

title: è¶ٹهچ— 2002 #12
problem_md:
Solve the equation:
$$
\sqrt{4 - 3\sqrt{10 - 3x}} = x - 2.
$$
solution_md:
The set of definition of the equation is $X = [74/27; 10/3]$.
On $X$, we have:
$$
\text{given equation} \Leftrightarrow 4 - 3\sqrt{10 - 3x} = x^2 - 4x + 4 \Leftrightarrow 9(10 - 3x) = x^2(4 - x)^2 \Leftrightarrow \\
x^4 - 8x^3 + 16x^2 + 27x - 90 = 0 \Leftrightarrow (x - 3)(x + 2)(x^2 - 7x + 15) = 0 \Leftrightarrow x = 3.
$$

problem_md: Find all triangles $ABC$ such that the angle $\angle BCA$ is acute, the perpendicular bisector of the segment $BC$ cuts the two rays dividing the angle $\angle BAC$ into three equal angles ($\angle BAx = \angle xAy = \angle yAC$) at the points $N$ and $P$ satisfying the conditions $AB = NP = 2HM$, where $H$ is the orthogonal projection of $A$ on $BC$ and $M$ is the midpoint of $BC$.

title: è¶ٹهچ— 2002 #14
problem_md: In the plane, let be given an isosceles triangle $ABC$ ($AB = AC$). A variable circle $(O)$ with center $O$ on the line $BC$, passes through $A$ but does not touch the lines $AB$, $AC$. Let $M$, $N$ be respectively the second points of intersection of the circle $(O)$ with the lines $AB$, $AC$. Find the locus of the orthocenter of triangle $AMN$.
solution_md:
1st case: $\angle A = 90^\circ$: the locus is the singleton $\{A\}$.

2nd case: $\angle A \neq 90^\circ$: let $D$ be the point symmetric to $A$ with respect to $BC$, $K$ be the point symmetric to $D$ with respect to $MN$ then the line $HK$ is the image of the line $BC$ under the homothety with center $D$ and ratio $4\sin^2(A/2)$. Thus the locus of the orthocenter of triangle $AMN$ is $d \setminus \{H_1, H_2\}$ where $d$ is the image of $BC$ under the above mentioned homothety; $H_1, H_2$ are the points on $d$ such that $DH_1 \perp DB$, $DH_2 \perp DC$.

title: è¶ٹهچ— ???? #15
problem_md:
A positive integer $N_0$ is written on the board. Two persons, $A$ and $B$, play the following game: $A$ erases $N_0$ and writes a number $N_1 \in \{N_0 - 1; [N_0/3]\}$. Then $B$ erases $N_1$ and writes a number $N_2 \in \{N_1 - 1; [N_1/3]\}$. At his turn, $A$ erases $N_2$ and so on. They continue to play until on the board appears the number $0$; the player who writes first the number $0$ is considered as the winner and the other player is considered lost.

Determine whether the player $A$ or the player $B$ has a strategy to guarantee his win when:
$$
1/\ N_0 = 120 ?
$$
$$
2/\ N_0 = (3^{2002} - 1)/2 ?
$$
$$
3/\ N_0 = (3^{2002} + 1)/2 ?
$$

title: è¶ٹهچ— ???? #16
è¶ٹهچ— 2002 #17
problem_md: Let be given two positive integers $m$, $n$ with $m < 2001$, $n < 2002$; and let be given $2001 \times 2002$ distinct real numbers. Put these numbers into the little squares of a rectangular board of size $2001 \times 2002$ (the board consists of $2001$ rows and $2002$ columns) so that each number is putting in a little square and each little square is filling with a number. We call a little square "bad" if the number put in it is less than at least $m$ numbers put in the same column as the considered little square and simultaneously this number is less than at least $n$ numbers put in the same row as the considered little square. For each such filling, let $s$ be the number of "bad" little squares. Find the least value of $s$.
solution_md:
We enlarge the problem by replacing $2001$ by $p$, $2002$ by $q$ ($m \le p, n \le q$) and prove by induction on $p+q$ that $s \ge (p-m)(q-n)$ (1).
It is easily seen that the assertion (1) is true for $p+q=2, 3, 4$. Suppose that it is true for $p+q=k$.
Consider a $(p, q)$-board with $p+q=k+1$. It is easy to see that (1) is true if $m=p$ or $n=q$. Consider the case where $m<p$ and $n<q$. We call a little square of the board is bad by row or row-bad (resp. bad by column or column-bad) if the number written in this little square is less than at least $n$ numbers (resp. $m$ numbers) written on the same row (resp. column) as the considered little square.
If in the $(p, q)$-board, each row-bad little square is also a column-bad one and each column-bad little square is also a row-bad little square one then it is easily seen that $s = (p-m)(q-n)$, thus (1) is true. Suppose that in the $(p, q)$-board there are little squares that are bad only by row or bad only by column. Let $a$ be the least number written in these little squares. Suppose w. l. o. g. that the little square filling by $a$ is row-bad. Then all $(p-m)$ column-bad little squares lying on the same column as $a$ are bad little squares of the board. By erasing the column containing the little square filling by $a$, we obtain a $(p, q-1)$-board such that a little square of which is bad if and only if it is bad in the previous $(p, q)$-board. Because $p+(q-1)=k$, by induction, we deduce that the number of bad little squares in the $(p, q-1)$-board is not less than $(p-m)(q-1-n)$. Therefore, the number of bad little squares in the $(p, q)$-board is not less than
$$
(p - m)(q - 1 - n) + (p - m) = (p - m)(q - n).
$$
Thus the assertion (1) is proved.
Now we indicate a filling in the $(p, q)$-board such that the number of bad little squares equals $(p-m)(q-n)$: arrange $p \times q$ given numbers in increasing order and put them one after another into the little squares of the board downwards and from left to right.
$$
\text{Thus } s_{\min} = (p - m)(q - n) \text{ and the answer to the given problem is } (2001 - m)(2002 - n)
$$

title: è¶ٹهچ— 2002 #18
problem_md:
Let $a$, $b$, $c$ be real numbers such that the polynomial $P(x) = x^3 + a x^2 + b x + c$ has three real roots (not necessarily distinct).
Prove that:
$$
12ab + 27c \leq 6a^3 + 10(a^2 - 2b)^{3/2}.
$$
When does equality occur?
solution_md:
The demanded inequality can be written in the form:
$$
-6a(a^2 - 2b) \leq -27c + 10(a^2 - 2b)^{3/2} \quad (1)
$$
Let $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ be three real roots of the polynomial $P(x)$. By the theorem of Viet, (1) is equivalent to
$$
6(\alpha + \beta + \gamma)(\alpha^2 + \beta^2 + \gamma^2) \leq 27\alpha\beta\gamma + 10(\alpha^2 + \beta^2 + \gamma^2)^{3/2} \quad (2)
$$
If $\alpha^2 + \beta^2 + \gamma^2 = 0$ then (2) is evident and in this case, (2) is an equality.
If $\alpha^2 + \beta^2 + \gamma^2 > 0$, w. l. o. g. we can suppose that
$$
|\alpha| \leq |\beta| \leq |\gamma| \quad (*)
$$
$$
\text{and} \quad \alpha^2 + \beta^2 + \gamma^2 = 9 \quad (**)
$$
Then (2) is equivalent to
$$
2(\alpha + \beta + \gamma) - \alpha\beta\gamma \leq 10 \quad (3)
$$
We deduce from (*) and (**) that
$$
\begin{aligned}
[2(\alpha + \beta + \gamma) - \alpha\beta\gamma]^2 &= [2(\alpha + \beta) + \gamma(2 - \alpha\beta)]^2 \\
&\leq [(\alpha + \beta)^2 + \gamma^2][4 + (2 - \alpha\beta)^2] \\
&= (9 + 2\alpha\beta)[8 - 4\alpha\beta + (\alpha\beta)^2] \\
&= 2(\alpha\beta)^3 + (\alpha\beta)^2 - 20\alpha\beta + 72 \\
&= (\alpha\beta + 2)^2(2\alpha\beta - 7) + 100
\end{aligned} \quad (4)
$$
But from (*) and (**), we see that $\gamma^2 \geq 3$, therefore $2\alpha\beta \leq \alpha^2 + \beta^2 = 9 - \gamma^2 \leq 6$ and (4) gives:
$$
[2(\alpha + \beta + \gamma) - \alpha\beta\gamma]^2 \leq 100,
$$
so (3) is proved.
(4) is an equality when and only when
$$
\begin{cases}
|\alpha| \leq |\beta| \leq |\gamma| \\
\alpha^2 + \beta^2 + \gamma^2 = 9 \\
(\alpha + \beta)/2 = \gamma/2 - \alpha\beta \\
\alpha\beta + 2 = 0 \\
2(\alpha + \beta + \gamma) - \alpha\beta\gamma \geq 0
\end{cases}
$$
therefore it occurs when and only when $\alpha = -1$ and $\beta = \gamma = 2$.
Thus (2) is an equality when and only when $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$ is a permutation of $(-t, 2t, 2t)$, where $t$ is a non negative real number, so (1) is an equality when and only when
$$
a = -3t, \quad b = 0 \text{ and } c = 4t^3,
$$
where $t$ is a non negative real number.

title: è¶ٹهچ— 2002 #20
problem_md:
Find all positive integer $n$ such that the equation
$$
x + y + u + v = n\sqrt{xyuv}
$$
has positive integer solution $x$, $y$, $u$, $v$.
solution_md:
For $x$, $y$, $u$, $v \in \mathbb{Z}^+$, we can write the given equation in the form
$$
(x + y + u + v)^2 = n^2xyuv
$$
or in the form
$$
x^2 + 2(y + u + v)x + (y + u + v)^2 = n^2xyuv \quad (1)
$$
Let $n$ be a positive integer such that (1) has positive integer solutions $(x, y, u, v)$. Let $(x_0, y_0, u_0, v_0)$ be a positive integer solution of (1) such that $x_0 + y_0 + u_0 + v_0$ has the least value. We can suppose w. l. o. g. that $x_0 \geq y_0 \geq u_0 \geq v_0$. It is easily seen that:
$$
f(x) = x^2 - n^2 y_0^2 u_0 v_0 x + 2(y_0 + u_0 + v_0)x + (y_0 + u_0 + v_0)^2.
$$
From (i), (ii) and the theorem of Viet, we deduce that $x_1 = (y_0 + u_0 + v_0)^2/x_0$ is also a positive integer root of $f(x)$. Thus $(x_1, y_0, u_0, v_0)$ is a positive integer solution of (1). From the definition of the root $(x_0, y_0, u_0, v_0)$, we have:
$$
x_1 \ge x_0 \ge y_0 \ge u_0 \ge v_0. \quad (2)
$$
The theorem on the sign of polynomial of second degree gives:
$$
0 \le f(y_0) = y_0^2 - n^2 y_0^2 u_0 v_0 + 2(y_0 + u_0 + v_0)y_0 + (y_0 + u_0 + v_0)^2 \le 16y_0^2 - n^2 y_0^2 u_0 v_0 \\
\text{therefore } n^2 y_0^2 u_0 v_0 \le 16y_0^2, \text{ so } n^2 \le n^2 u_0 v_0 \le 16 \text{ and } n \in \{1; 2; 3; 4\}.
$$
$(x, y, u, v) = (4, 4, 4, 4), (2, 2, 2, 2), (1, 1, 2, 2), (1, 1, 1, 1)$ are respectively positive integer solutions of (1) for $n = 1, 2, 3, 4$.
So the demanded values of $n$ are $1, 2, 3, 4$.

title: è¶ٹهچ— 2002 #22
problem_md:
Consider the equation
$$
\frac{1}{x-1} + \frac{1}{4x-1} + \dots + \frac{1}{k^2x-1} + \dots + \frac{1}{n^2x-1} = \frac{1}{2}
$$
where $n$ is a positive integer parameter.
1/ Prove that for every positive integer $n$, the considered equation has a unique root greater than $1$, which is denoted by $x_n$.
2/ Prove that the sequence $\{x_n\}$ has limit $4$ (when $n$ tends to $+\infty$).
solution_md:
The given equation can be written in the form:
$$
f_n(x) = \frac{-1}{2} + \frac{1}{x-1} + \frac{1}{4x-1} + \dots + \frac{1}{k^2x-1} + \dots + \frac{1}{n^2x-1} = 0 \quad (1)
$$
1/ It is easily seen that for every $n \in \mathbb{N}^*$, the function $f_n(x)$ is continuous and decreasing on the interval $(1; +\infty)$. Moreover, $f_n(x) \to +\infty$ when $x \to 1^+$ and $f_n(x) \to -1/2$ when $x \to +\infty$. Therefore, for every $n \in \mathbb{N}^*$, the equation (1) has a unique root $x_n > 1$.

2/ For $n \in \mathbb{N}^*$, we have:
$$
\begin{aligned}
f_n(4) &= \frac{-1}{2} + \frac{1}{2^2-1} + \frac{1}{4^2-1} + \dots + \frac{1}{(2k)^2-1} + \dots + \frac{1}{(2n)^2-1} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \left( -1 + 1 - \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{3} - \frac{1}{5} + \dots + \frac{1}{2k-1} - \frac{1}{2k+1} + \dots + \frac{1}{2n-1} - \frac{1}{2n+1} \right) \\
&= \frac{-1}{2(2n+1)} < 0 = f_n(x_n).
\end{aligned}
$$
But $f_n(x)$ is decreasing on $(1; +\infty)$, so
$$
x_n < 4 \quad \forall n \in \mathbb{N}^*. \quad (2)
$$
On the other hand, because for every $n \in \mathbb{N}^*$, the function $f_n(x)$ is differentiable on the interval $(1; +\infty) \supset |x_n; 4|$, the theorem of Lagrange gives: for every $n \in \mathbb{N}^*$, there exists $t \in (x_n; 4)$ such that
$$
\frac{f_n(4) - f_n(x_n)}{4 - x_n} = f_n'(t) = \frac{-1}{(t-1)^2} + \frac{-4}{(4t-1)^2} + \dots + \frac{-n^2}{(n^2t-1)^2} < \frac{-1}{9}
$$
$$
\text{so} \quad \frac{-1}{2(2n + 1)(4 - x_n)} < \frac{-1}{9}
$$
$$
\text{i.e.} \quad x_n > 4 - \frac{9}{2(2n + 1)} \quad \forall n \in \mathbb{N}^*
$$

Thus, $\forall n \in N^*, \quad 4 - \frac{9}{2(2n + 1)} < x_n < 4$.
The squeeze theorem proves that $\lim_{n \to \infty} x_n = 4$.

title: è¶ٹهچ— 2003 #24
problem_md:
Let be given an acute triangle $ABC$ inscribed in a circle with center $O$ and two points $M$, $N$ on the line $AC$ such that $\overline{MN} = \overline{AC}$. Let $D$ be the orthogonal projection of $M$ on the line $BC$, $E$ be that of $N$ on the line $AB$.

1/ Prove that the orthocenter $H$ of triangle $ABC$ lies on the circumcircle with the center $O'$ of triangle $BED$.

2/ Prove that the midpoint of segment $AN$ is symmetric to $B$ with respect to the midpoint of segment $OO'$.
solution_md:
1/ Let $K$ be the point of intersection of $MD$ and $NE$. It is clear that the circle with diameter $BK$ circumscribes about $\triangle BED$.

We have: $AH \parallel MK$ and $CH \parallel NK$.

It implies: $\angle HAC = \angle KMN$ and $\angle ACH = \angle MNK$.

From these equalities and from $AC = MN$, we see that $\triangle AHC \cong \triangle MKN$. Thus $d(K, AC) = d(H, AC)$. But $K$ and $H$ lie on the same side with respect to the line $AC$, it shows that $KH \parallel AC$. Therefore $KH \perp BH$ and $H$ lies on the circle with diameter $BK$ circumscribing about $\triangle BED$.

2/ From the proof of 1/, it is clear that $O'$ is the midpoint of segment $BK$. Let $I$ be the midpoint of segment $AN$. It is easy to see that the orthogonal projections of $I$ on the line $BA$ and on the line $BC$ are respectively the midpoints of $EA$ and $DC$. Therefore, the orthogonal projections of vector $O'M$ on the line $BA$ and on the line $BC$ are respectively $(1/2)\overrightarrow{BA}$ and $(1/2)\overrightarrow{BC}$. It shows that $\overrightarrow{OM} = \overrightarrow{BO}$. Thus, quadrilateral $BO'MO$ is a parallelogram and the points $B$ and $M$ are symmetric with respect to the midpoint of segment $OO'$.

Let be given four positive integers $m, n, p, q$, with $p < m$ and $q < n$. Take the four points $A(0; 0)$, $B(p; 0)$, $C(m; q)$ and $D(m; n)$ in the coordinate plane.

Consider the paths $f$ from $A$ to $D$ and the paths $g$ from $B$ to $C$ such that when going along $f$ or $g$, one goes only in the positive directions of the axes of coordinates and one can only change directions (from the positive direction of one axe of coordinate into the positive direction of the other axe of coordinate) at the points with integral coordinates.

Let $S$ be the number of couples $(f, g)$ such that $f$ and $g$ have no common point. Prove that
$$
S = C_{m+n}^{p} \cdot C_{m+q-p}^{q} - C_{m+q}^{p} \cdot C_{m+n-p}^{q}.
$$

title: è¶ٹهچ— 2003 #26
problem_md:
For each integer $n > 1$, denote by $s_n$ the number of permutations $(a_1, a_2, ..., a_n)$ of $n$ first positive integers such that each permutation satisfies the condition:
$$
1 \le |a_k - k| \le 2 \quad \text{for every } k = 1, 2, ..., n.
$$
Prove that: $1.75 \cdot s_{n-1} < s_n < 2 \cdot s_{n-1}$ for all integers $n > 6$.
solution_md:
â€¢ Firstly, we construct an inductive relation for $s_n$.
Consider an integer $n > 4$. Denote by $P_n$ the set of permutations $(a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n)$ satisfying the condition of the problem.
Consider the partition:
$$
P_n = \bigcup_{k=1}^{n} S_k \qquad (1)
$$
where $S_k$ is the set of permutations $(a_1, a_2, ..., a_n) \in P_n$ such that
$$
\{a_1, a_2, ..., a_k\} = \{1, 2, ..., k\},
$$
$$
\{a_1, a_2, ..., a_i\} \neq \{1, 2, ..., i\} \quad \forall i < k.
$$
For every $k > 1$, denote by $H_k$ the set of permutations $(a_1, a_2, ..., a_k)$ of $k$ first positive integers such that each permutation satisfies simultaneously the following two conditions:
$$
i/ \quad 1 \le |a_i - i| \le 2 \quad \forall i = 1, 2, ..., k; \qquad (2)
$$
$$
ii/ \quad \{a_1, a_2, ..., a_i\} \neq \{1, 2, ..., i\} \quad \forall i = 1, 2, ..., k-1. \qquad (3)
$$
Put $t_k = |H_k|$. Then it is easy to see that for every $k = 1, 2, ..., n$, we have $|S_k| = t_k \cdot S_{n-k}$. (We put $S_0 = 1$). It follows from (1) that
$$
S_n = |P_n| = t_1 \cdot S_{n-1} + t_2 \cdot S_{n-2} + ... + t_n \cdot S_0 \qquad (4)
$$
â€¢ Secondly, we calculate $t_k$, $k = 1, 2, ..., n$.
It is easy to see that:
$$
H_1 = \emptyset \Rightarrow t_1 = 0. \qquad (5)
$$
$$
H_2 = \{(2, 1)\} \Rightarrow t_2 = 1. \qquad (6)
$$
$$
H_3 = \{(2, 3, 1); (3, 1, 2)\} \Rightarrow t_3 = 2. \qquad (7)
$$
$$
H_4 = \{(2, 4, 1, 3); (3, 1, 4, 2); (3, 4, 1, 2)\} \Rightarrow t_4 = 3. \qquad (8)
$$
Consider $H_k$ with $4 < k \le n$.
Let $(a_1, a_2, ..., a_k) \in H_k$. Can occur:
- 1st case: $a_1 = 2$.
We shall prove that in this case, $a_{2i} = 2i + 2$ and $a_{2i+1} = 2i - 1$ (*) for every $i = 1, 2, ..., [k/2] - 1$.
Indeed, it follows from (2) and (3) that $a_3 = 1$; and so, from (3), $a_2 \neq 3$. It follows that $a_2 = 4$ (since $a_2 \in \{1, 3, 4\}$ from (2)). So (*) holds for $i = 1$.
Suppose that (*) holds for every $i \le m$, with $1 \le m \le [k/2] - 2$, i.e. we have:
$$
a_2 = 4, a_3 = 1, ..., a_{2m-2} = 2m, a_{2m-1} = 2m-3, a_{2m} = 2m+2, a_{2m+1} = 2m \qquad (9)
$$
From (9) and (2) it follows that $2m + 1 \in \{a_{2m+2}, a_{2m+3}\}$.
If $a_{2m+2} = 2m+1$ then with remarking that $j \in \{a_1, a_2, ..., a_{2m+1}\}$ for every $j < 2m$, we get
$$
\{a_1, a_2, ..., a_{2m+2}\} = \{1, 2, ..., 2m+2\},
$$
which contradicts (3) (since $2m + 2 < k$).
So $a_{2m+3}$ must be equal to $2m+1$. (**).
From (**), (2) and (9) it follows that $a_{2m+2} \in \{2m+3, 2m+4\}$. But if $a_{2m+2} = 2m+3$, by an analogous reasoning, we get
$$
\{a_1, a_2, ..., a_{2m+3}\} = \{1, 2, ..., 2m+3\},
$$
which contradicts (3). So $a_{2m+2}$ must be equal to $2m+4$.
These results show that (*) holds for $i = m + 1$. So, by induction, (*) holds for all $i = 1, 2, ..., [k/2] - 1$.
By an analogous reasoning, we have:
$$
+ a_k = k - 1 \text{ if k is even, and}
$$
+ $a_{k-1} = k$, $a_k = k-2$ if k is odd.
Thus, there is a unique permutation $(a_1, a_2, ..., a_k) \in H_k$ such that $a_1 = 2$.
- 2nd case: $a_1 = 3$.
In this case, we shall prove analogously that $(3, a_2, ..., a_k) \in H_k \Leftrightarrow a_2 = 1$ and
+ if k is even then: $a_{2i-1} = 2i+1$, $a_{2i} = 2i-2$ $\forall i = 2, ..., \lfloor k/2 \rfloor - 1$; $a_{k-1} = k$, $a_k = k-2$.
+ if k is odd then: $a_{2i-1} = 2i+1$, $a_{2i} = 2i-2$ $\forall i = 2, ..., \lfloor k/2 \rfloor$; $a_k = k-1$.
Shortly, we have: $t_k = 2$ $\forall k = 4, 5, ..., n$. (10)
â€¢ From (4) â€“ (8) and (10) it follows that:
$$
S_n = S_{n-2} + 2 \cdot S_{n-3} + 3 \cdot S_{n-4} + 2(S_{n-5} + \dots + S_1 + S_0) \quad (11)
$$
Therefore
$$
S_{n+1} = S_{n-1} + 2 \cdot S_{n-2} + 3 \cdot S_{n-3} + 2(S_{n-4} + \dots + S_1 + S_0) \quad (12)
$$
By subtracting (11) from (12), we get
$$
S_{n+1} - S_n = S_{n-1} + S_{n-2} + S_{n-3} - S_{n-4} \quad (13)
$$
hence
$$
S_{n+2} - S_{n+1} = S_n + S_{n-1} + S_{n-2} - S_{n-3} \quad (14)
$$
By subtracting (13) from (14), we get
$$
S_{n+2} - 2 \cdot S_{n+1} = S_{n-4} - 2 \cdot S_{n-3}.
$$
$$
\text{So, with } n > 6, \text{ we have: } S_n - 2 \cdot S_{n-1} = S_{n-6} - 2 \cdot S_{n-5}. \quad (15)
$$
It is easy to prove that $S_{n-5} > S_{n-6} \quad \forall n > 6$. Therefore, from (15), we get:
$$
S_n < 2 \cdot S_{n-1} \quad \forall n > 6. \quad (16)
$$
$$
\text{Moreover, from (13) we have } S_n > S_{n-1} + S_{n-2} + S_{n-3} \quad \forall n > 5 \quad (17)
$$
From (16), (17) it follows that for every $n > 8$, we have:
$$
S_n > S_{n-1} + (S_{n-1/2} + S_{n-2/2}) > S_{n-1} + (S_{n-1/2} + S_{n-1/4}) = 1.75 \cdot S_{n-1}
$$
By counting directly, we have $S_1 = 0$, $S_2 = 1$, $S_3 = 2$, $S_4 = 4$. Then, from (14) we can calculate: $S_5 = 6$, $S_6 = 13$, $S_7 = 24$, $S_8 = 45$. These results show that $S_7 > 1.75 \cdot S_6$ and $S_8 > 1.75 \cdot S_7$. Therefore $S_n > 1.75 \cdot S_{n-1} \quad \forall n > 6$.
$$
\text{So: } 1.75 \cdot S_{n-1} < S_n < 2 \cdot S_{n-1} \quad \forall n > 6.
$$

title: è¶ٹهچ— 2003 #27
problem_md: In plane, let be given two fixed circles $ (O_1) $ and $ (O_2) $ touching each other at $ M $, the radius of $ (O_2) $ is greater than that of $ (O_1) $. $ A $ is the point on $ (O_2) $ such that the points $ O_1 $, $ O_2 $, $ A $ are not collinear. Let $ AB $ and $ AC $ be the tangents to $ (O_1) $ with touching points $ B $ and $ C $. The lines $ MB $ and $ MC $ cut again $ (O_2) $ respectively at $ E $ and $ F $. Let $ D $ be the point of intersection of the line $ EF $ and tangent to $ (O_2) $ at $ A $. Prove that $ D $ moves on a fixed line when $ A $ moves on $ (O_2) $ so that the three points $ O_1 $, $ O_2 $, $ A $ are not collinear.
solution_md:
We consider two cases:

* **First case:** The circles $ (O_1), (O_2) $ touch each other externally at $ M $.
Let $ xy $ be the common tangent at $ M $ of $ (O_1), (O_2) $. Since $ CA $ and $ My $ are tangents to $ (O_1) $ at $ C $ and $ M $, we have $ \angle FCA = \angle CMy $. But $ \angle CMy = \angle FMx $, hence $ \angle FCA = \angle FMx $. As $ \angle FMx = \angle FAM $ it follows that $ \angle FCA = \angle FAM $.
The triangles $ MFA $ and $ AFC $ have: $ \angle MFA = \angle AFC $ and $ \angle FAM = \angle FCA $, so they are similar. Therefore $ \frac{MF}{FA} = \frac{AF}{FC} $ t.c. $ FM \cdot FC = FA^2 $. But $ FM \cdot FC = P_M(O_1) $ (the power of $ M $ with respect to the circle $ (O_1) $) $ = FO_1^2 - R_1^2 $, where $ R_1 $ is the radius of $ (O_1) $, it follows that $ FO_1^2 - FA^2 = R_1^2 $. Analogously, we have $ EO_1^2 - EA^2 = R_1^2 $. So
$$
FO_1^2 - FA^2 = EO_1^2 - EA^2 = R_1^2.
$$
As $ D $ lies on the line $ EF $, it implies that $ DO_1^2 - DA^2 = R_1^2 $, i.e.

$$
DO_1^2 - R_1^2 = DA^2 \quad (1)
$$
Since $ DA $ is the tangent to $ (O_2) $ at $ A $, $ DA^2 = PD/(O_2) = DO_2^2 - R_2^2 $ (2), where $ R_2 $ is the radius of $ (O_2) $.
From (1) and (2), we get $ DO_1^2 - R_1^2 = DO_2^2 - R_2^2 $, or $ PD/(O_1) = PD/(O_2) $. It shows that $ D $ lies on the radical line of $ (O_1) $ and $ (O_2) $.

* 2<sup>d</sup> case: The circles $ (O_1), (O_2) $ touch each other internally at $ M $.
The proof in this case is analogous to the proof in the 1st case.

title: è¶ٹهچ— 2003 #29
problem_md:
Find all polynomials $P(x)$ with real coefficients, satisfying the relation
$$
(x^3 + 3x^2 + 3x + 2)P(x - 1) = (x^3 - 3x^2 + 3x - 2)P(x)
$$
for every real number $x$.
solution_md:
We have:
$$
(x^3 + 3x^2 + 3x + 2)P(x - 1) = (x^3 - 3x^2 + 3x - 2)P(x) \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R} \quad (1)
$$
$$
\Leftrightarrow (x + 2)(x^2 + x + 1)P(x - 1) = (x - 2)(x^2 - x + 1)P(x) \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R} \quad (2)
$$
By substituting $x = -2$ into (2), we get $0 = -28 \cdot P(-2)$, so $P(-2) = 0$.
By substituting $x = 2$ into (2), we get $28 \cdot P(1) = 0$, so $P(1) = 0$.
Therefore,
- By substituting $x = -1$ into (1), we get $0 = -9 \cdot P(-1)$, so $P(-1) = 0$.
- By substituting $x = 1$ into (1), we get $9 \cdot P(0) = 0$, so $P(0) = 0$.
From these results, it follows that
$$
P(x) = (x - 1)x(x + 1)(x + 2)Q(x) \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}, \quad (3)
$$
where $Q(x)$ is a polynomial in $x$ with real coefficients.
$$
\text{It implies that } P(x - 1) = (x - 2)(x - 1)x(x + 1)Q(x - 1) \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}. \quad (4)
$$
From (2), (3) and (4), we get:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& (x - 2)(x - 1)x(x + 1)(x + 2)(x^2 + x + 1)Q(x - 1) \\
& = (x - 2)(x - 1)x(x + 1)(x + 2)(x^2 - x + 1)Q(x) \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}.
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
\text{It follows that } (x^2 + x + 1)Q(x - 1) = (x^2 - x + 1)Q(x) \quad \forall x \neq 0, \pm 1, \pm 2.
$$
As each side of this equality is a polynomial in $x$, we get
$$
(x^2 + x + 1)Q(x - 1) = (x^2 - x + 1)Q(x) \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}. \quad (5)
$$
Since $(x^2 + x + 1, x^2 - x + 1) = 1$, it implies that
$$
Q(x) = (x^2 + x + 1)R(x) \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}, \quad (6)
$$
where $R(x)$ is a polynomial in $x$ with real coefficients, and so
$$
Q(x - 1) = (x^2 - x + 1)R(x - 1), \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}. \quad (7)
$$
From (5), (6) and (7), we get:
$$
(x^2 + x + 1)(x^2 - x + 1)R(x - 1) = (x^2 - x + 1)(x^2 + x + 1)R(x) \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}.
$$
But $(x^2 - x + 1)(x^2 + x + 1) \neq 0 \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}$, we have $R(x - 1) = R(x) \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}$, so $R(x)$ is a constant, and from (6), (3) we get:
$$
P(x) = c(x - 1)x(x + 1)(x + 2)(x^2 + x + 1) \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R},
$$
where $c$ is an arbitrary real constant.
A direct verification and shows that these polynomials satisfy the given relation and thus they are polynomials sought for.

title: è¶ٹهچ— ???? #30
problem_md:
Let $f: N \times Z \to N$ be a given function satisfying simultaneously the following conditions:
i/ $f(0, 0) = 5^{2003}$, $f(0, n) = 0$ for every integer $n \neq 0$;
ii/ $f(m, n) = f(m - 1, n) - 2\left[\frac{f(m - 1, n)}{2}\right] + \left[\frac{f(m - 1, n - 1)}{2}\right] + \left[\frac{f(m - 1, n + 1)}{2}\right]$ for every natural number $m > 0$ and for every integer $n$.
Prove that there exists natural number $M$ such that
$f(M, n) = 1$ for all integers $n$ such that $|n| \le (5^{2003} - 1)/2$
and $f(M, n) = 0$ for all integers $n$ such that $|n| > (5^{2003} - 1)/2$.

problem_md:
On the sides of triangle $ABC$ take the points $M_1$, $N_1$, $P_1$ such that each line $MM_1$, $NN_1$, $PP_1$ divides the perimeter of $ABC$ into two equal parts ($M$, $N$, $P$ are respectively the midpoints of sides $BC$, $CA$, $AB$).

1/ Prove that the lines $MM_1$, $NN_1$, $PP_1$ are concurrent at a point $K$.

2/ Prove that among the ratios $\frac{KA}{BC}$, $\frac{KB}{CA}$, $\frac{KC}{AB}$ there exists at least a ratio which is not less than $\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$.

title: è¶ٹهچ— 2003 #32
problem_md:
Let be given two polynomials
$$
P(x) = 4x^3 - 2x^2 - 15x + 9
$$
and
$$ Q(x) = 12x^3 + 6x^2 - 7x + 1. $$
1/ Prove that each of these polynomials has three distinct real roots.
2/ Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be respectively the greatest roots of $P(x)$ and $Q(x)$. Prove that $\alpha^2 + 3\beta^2 = 4$.
solution_md:
1. We have:
$$
P(-2) = -1;\ P(-1) = 18;\ P(3/2) = -9/2 \text{ and } P(\sqrt{33}/3) = (15 - \sqrt{33})/9. \quad (1)
$$
$$
Q(-2) = -57;\ Q(-1) = 2;\ Q(1/3) = -2/9 \text{ and } Q(1) = 12. \quad (2)
$$
From (1) it follows that $P(x)$ has three distinct real roots, and from (2) it follows that $Q(x)$ has three distinct real roots.

2. As $\alpha$ is a root of $P(x)$,
$$
4\alpha^3 - 2\alpha^2 - 15\alpha + 9 = 0.
$$
Therefore $4\alpha^3 - 15\alpha = 2\alpha^2 - 9$, so
$$
16\alpha^6 - 120\alpha^4 + 225\alpha^2 = 4\alpha^4 - 36\alpha^2 + 81
$$
i.e.
$$
16\alpha^6 - 124\alpha^4 + 261\alpha^2 - 81 = 0.
$$
(3)
$$
\text{Since } \alpha \text{ is the greatest root of } P(x), \text{ from (1), we get: } \frac{\sqrt{33}}{3} > \alpha > \frac{3}{2}. \quad (4)
$$
and so $4 - \alpha^2 > 0$. We shall prove that $\frac{\sqrt{3(4 - \alpha^2)}}{3}$ is a root of $Q(x)$.
Indeed, we have:
$$
\begin{align*}
Q\left(\frac{\sqrt{3(4 - \alpha^2)}}{3}\right) = 0 &\Leftrightarrow \frac{4}{3}(4 - \alpha^2)\sqrt{3(4 - \alpha^2)} + 2(4 - \alpha^2) - \frac{7}{3}\sqrt{3(4 - \alpha^2)} + 1 = 0 \\
&\Leftrightarrow \left(3 - \frac{4\alpha^2}{3}\right)\sqrt{3(4 - \alpha^2)} + 9 - 2\alpha^2 = 0 \\
&\Leftrightarrow (9 - 2\alpha^2)^2 = \left(3 - \frac{4\alpha^2}{3}\right)^2 \cdot 3(4 - \alpha^2) \quad \text{(do (4))} \\
&\Leftrightarrow 3(81 - 36\alpha^2 + 4\alpha^4) = (81 - 72\alpha^2 + 16\alpha^4)(4 - \alpha^2) \\
&\Leftrightarrow 16\alpha^6 - 124\alpha^4 + 261\alpha^2 - 81 = 0.
\end{align*}
$$
(3) shows that the last equality is true, so $x_0 = \frac{\sqrt{3(4 - \alpha^2)}}{3}$ is a root of $Q(x)$.
Moreover, from (4) it is easy to see that $x_0 \in (\frac{1}{3}; \frac{\sqrt{21}}{6}) \subset (\frac{1}{3}; 1)$.
On the other hand, since $\beta$ is the greatest root of $Q(x)$, (2) shows that $\beta$ is the unique root of $Q(x)$ in the interval $(\frac{1}{3}; 1)$.
From these results, it follows that $\frac{\sqrt{3(4 - \alpha^2)}}{3} = \beta$, thus $\alpha^2 + 3\beta^2 = 4$.

title: è¶ٹهچ— ???? #33
problem_md:
Let $A$ be the set of all permutations $a = (a_1, a_2, ..., a_{2003})$ of $2003$ first positive integers such that each permutation satisfies the condition: there is no nonvoid proper subset $S$ of the set $\{1, 2, ..., 2003\}$ such that $\{a_k \mid k \in S\} = S$.

For each $a = (a_1, a_2, ..., a_{2003}) \in A$, put $d(a) = \sum_{k=1}^{2003} (a_k - k)^2$.

a. Find the least value of $d(a)$. Denote this least value by $d_0$.

b. Find all permutations $a \in A$ such that $d(a) = d_0$.

title: è¶ٹهچ— 2003 #34
problem_md:
Find the greatest positive integer $n$ such that the system of equations
$$
(x+1)^2 + y_1^2 = (x+2)^2 + y_2^2 = \dots = (x+k)^2 + y_k^2 = \dots = (x+n)^2 + y_n^2
$$
has integral solution $(x, y_1, y_2, \dots, y_n)$.
solution_md:
â€¢ First, it is easy to show the
*Lemma:* For arbitrary integers $a, b$, we have:
$$
a^2 + b^2 \equiv \begin{cases} 2; & 1 \equiv 5 \pmod{8} & \text{if } a \equiv \pm 1 \pmod{4} \\ 1; & 0 \equiv 4 \pmod{8} & \text{if } a \equiv 0 \pmod{4} \\ 5; & 4 \equiv 0 \pmod{8} & \text{if } a \equiv 2 \pmod{4} \end{cases}
$$
â€¢ Returning to the problem:
- For $n=3$: it is easily seen that the given system has integral solution $(x = -2; y_1 = 0; y_2 = 1; y_3 = 0)$.
- For $n=4$: Suppose that the given system has an integral solution $(x, y_1, y_2, y_3, y_4)$. Then, as $x+1, x+2, x+3, x+4$ form a complete system of residues mod 4, from the above mentioning lemma, there exists an integer $m$ such that
$$
m \in \{2; 1; 5\} \cap \{1; 0; 4\} \cap \{5; 4; 0\} = \emptyset.
$$
This contradiction proves that the given system has no integral solution when $n=4$. And then it is evident that the given system of equations has no integral solution for every $n \ge 4$.
- So $n=3$ is the greatest integer sought for.

title: è¶ٹهچ— ???? #35
problem_md: Let $n$ be a positive integer. Prove that the number $2^n + 1$ has no prime divisor of the form $8k-1$, where $k$ is a positive integer.

title: è¶ٹهچ— 2003 #36
problem_md:
Let $f$ be a function defined on the set of real numbers $\mathbb{R}$, taking values in $\mathbb{R}$ and satisfying the condition
$$
f(\cot x) = \sin 2x + \cos 2x
$$
for every $x$ belonging to the open interval $(0; \pi)$.
Find the least and the greatest values of the function $g(x) = f(x) \cdot f(1-x)$ on the closed interval $[-1; 1]$.
solution_md:
We have:
$$
f(\cot x) = \sin 2x + \cos 2x \quad \forall x \in (0; \pi)
$$
$$
\Leftrightarrow f(\cot x) = \frac{2 \cot x}{\cot^2 x + 1} + \frac{\cot^2 x - 1}{\cot^2 x + 1} = \frac{\cot^2 x + 2 \cot x - 1}{\cot^2 x + 1} \quad \forall x \in (0; \pi).
$$
Therefore, remarking that for every $t \in \mathbb{R}$ there exists $x \in (0; \pi)$ such that $\cot x = t$, we get:
$$
f(t) = \frac{t^2 + 2t - 1}{t^2 + 1} \quad \forall t \in \mathbb{R}.
$$
It implies
$$
g(x) = f(x) \cdot f(1-x) = \frac{x^2(1-x)^2 + 8x(1-x) - 2}{x^2(1-x)^2 - 2x(1-x) + 2} \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}. \quad (*)
$$
Put $u = x(1-x)$. It is easy to see that when $x$ runs through $[-1; 1]$, $u$ runs through $[-2; 1/4]$. So, from (*) we have:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\min g(x) = \min h(u) \quad \text{and} \quad \max g(x) = \max h(u), \\
-1 \le x \le 1 \qquad -2 \le u \le 1/4 \qquad -1 \le x \le 1 \qquad -2 \le u \le 1/4 \end{array}
$$
where $h(u) = \frac{u^2 + 8u - 2}{u^2 - 2u + 2}$.
By studying the sign of $h'(u) = \frac{2(-5u^2 + 4u + 6)}{(u^2 - 2u + 2)^2}$ on $[-2; 1/4]$, we get:
$$
\min_{-2 \le u \le 1/4} h(u) = h((2 - \sqrt{34})/5) = 4 - \sqrt{34}, \text{ and}
$$
$$
\max_{-2 \le u \le 1/4} h(u) = \max\{h(-2), h(1/4)\} = \max\{-7/5, 1/25\} = 1/25.
$$
So, on $[-1; 1]$, $\min g(x) = 4 - \sqrt{34}$, $\max g(x) = 1/25$.

title: è¶ٹهچ— 2003 #37
problem_md:
Let be given a real number $\alpha \neq 0$. Consider the sequence of real numbers $\{x_n\}$, $n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$, defined by:
$$
x_1 = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad x_{n+1}(x_n + \alpha) = \alpha + 1 \quad \text{for every } n = 1, 2, 3, \dots
$$
1/ Find the general term of the sequence $\{x_n\}$.
2/ Prove that the sequence $\{x_n\}$ has a finite limit when $n \to +\infty$. Find this limit.
solution_md:
1/ â€¢ For $\alpha = -1$, it is easily seen that $x_n = 0 \quad \forall n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$.
â€¢ For $\alpha \neq -1$, we have $x_n \neq -\alpha \quad \forall n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$, so we can write the inductive relation defining the sequence $\{x_n\}$ in the equivalent form:
$$
x_{n+1} = \frac{\alpha + 1}{x_n + \alpha} \quad \forall n = 1, 2, 3, \dots \quad (*)
$$
From $x_1 = 0$, (*) yields $x_2 = \frac{\alpha + 1}{\alpha}$.
Put $p_1 = 0$, $p_2 = \alpha + 1$, $q_1 = 1$, $q_2 = \alpha$, then $x_1 = \frac{p_1}{q_1}$, $x_2 = \frac{p_2}{q_2}$. Suppose that $x_k$ has the form $x_k = \frac{p_k}{q_k}$, then (*) shows that $x_{k+1}$ has the form $x_{k+1} = \frac{p_{k+1}}{q_{k+1}}$, where $p_{k+1} = (\alpha + 1)q_k$ and $q_{k+1} = \alpha q_k + p_k$. Therefore, by induction, $x_n$ has the form $x_n = \frac{p_n}{q_n} \quad \forall n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$, where $\{p_n\}$ and $\{q_n\}$ are defined by:
$$
p_1 = 0, q_1 = 1, q_2 = \alpha, \quad p_{n+1} = (\alpha + 1)q_n, \quad q_{n+1} = \alpha q_n + p_n \quad \forall n = 1, 2, 3, \dots
$$
From these relations, it follows that the sequence $\{q_n\}$ is defined by:
$$
q_1 = 1, q_2 = \alpha \quad \text{and} \quad q_{n+1} = \alpha q_n + (\alpha + 1)q_{n-1} \quad \forall n = 2, 3, \dots
$$
The characteristic equation of the sequence $\{q_n\}$ is
$$
x^2 - \alpha x - (\alpha + 1) = 0.
$$
This equation has two roots: $x_1 = -1$ and $x_2 = \alpha + 1$. Therefore:
$$
\text{- If } \alpha = -2 \text{ then } q_n = (-1)^{n-1} + (-1)^{n-1}(n-1) \quad \forall n = 1, 2, 3, \dots
$$
$$
\text{- If } \alpha \neq -2 \text{ then } q_n = \frac{(-1)^{n-1} + (\alpha+1)^n}{\alpha+2} \quad \forall n = 1, 2, 3, \dots
$$
and so:
$$
\text{- If } \alpha = -2 \text{ then } p_1 = 0, \quad p_n = -[((-1)^{n-2} + (-1)^{n-2})(n-2)] \quad \forall n \ge 2.
$$
- If $\alpha \neq -2$ then $p_1 = 0$, $p_n = \frac{(-1)^{n-2} + (\alpha + 1)^{n-1}}{\alpha + 2} (\alpha + 1) \quad \forall n \geq 2$.
Consequently:
- If $\alpha = -2$ then $x_n = \frac{n-1}{n} \quad \forall n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$.
- If $\alpha \neq -2$ then $x_n = \frac{|(-1)^{n-2} + (\alpha + 1)^{n-1}|(\alpha + 1)}{(-1)^{n-1} + (\alpha + 1)^n} \quad \forall n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$.

2/ â€¢ It is easy to see that:
- If $\alpha = -1$ then $\lim x_n = 0$.
- If $\alpha = -2$ then $\lim x_n = 1$.
â€¢ When $\alpha \neq -2$, we have:
$$
x_{2k-1} = \frac{(\alpha + 1)^{2k-1} - (\alpha + 1)}{1 + (\alpha + 1)^{2k-1}} \quad \forall k \neq 1, 2, 3, \dots
$$
$$
x_{2k} = \frac{(\alpha + 1) + (\alpha + 1)^{2k}}{(\alpha + 1)^{2k} - 1} \quad \forall k = 1, 2, 3, \dots
$$
It follows that:
- If $|\alpha + 1| > 1$ then $\lim x_{2k-1} = 1$ and $\lim x_{2k} = 1$.
- If $|\alpha + 1| < 1$ then $\lim x_{2k-1} = -(\alpha + 1)$ and $\lim x_{2k} = -(\alpha + 1)$.
In consequence, the sequence $\{x_n\}$ is convergent and:
- If $|\alpha + 1| > 1$ then $\lim x_n = 1$.
- If $|\alpha + 1| < 1$ then $\lim x_n = -(\alpha + 1)$.

title: è¶ٹهچ— 2003 #38
problem_md:
Let $F$ be the set of all functions $f: \mathbb{R}^+ \to \mathbb{R}^+$ satisfying the condition
$f(3x) \geq f(f(2x)) + x$ for every real positive number $x$.
Find the greatest real number $\alpha$ such that for all $f \in F$, we have
$$
f(x) \geq \alpha x
$$
for every real positive number $x$.
solution_md:
â€¢ It is clear that the function $f(x) = x/2$, $x \in \mathbb{R}^+$, is a function belonging to $F$. Thus $\alpha \leq 1/2$.
â€¢ Let $f$ be an arbitrary function in $F$. It is easy to see that
$$
f(x) \geq x/3 \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}^+. \qquad (1)
$$
Consider the sequence of numbers $\{\alpha_n\}$ defined by:
$$
\alpha_1 = 1/3 \quad \text{and} \quad \alpha_{n+1} = (2\alpha_n^2 + 1)/3 \quad \forall n = 1, 2, 3, \dots
$$
By induction on $n$, we shall prove that $\forall n \in \mathbb{N}^+$, we have
$$
f(x) \geq \alpha_n x \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}^+. \qquad (2)
$$
Indeed, (1) shows that we have (2) when $n = 1$.
Suppose that (2) holds for $n = k$. Then:
$$
f(x) \geq \alpha_k f(2x/3) + (x/3) \geq \alpha_k \cdot \alpha_k(2x/3) + (x/3) = ((2\alpha_k^2 + 1)/3)x = \alpha_{k+1}x \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}^+
$$
so (2) holds for $n = k + 1$. So, by induction, (2) is true.
Now we shall prove that $\lim \alpha_n = 1/2$.
Indeed, at first, by induction on $n$, it is easy to prove that the sequence $\{\alpha_n\}$ is bounded above by $1/2$. Therefore,
$$
\alpha_{n+1} - \alpha_n = (1/3)(\alpha_n - 1)(2\alpha_n - 1) > 0,
$$
it shows that $\{\alpha_n\}$ is an increasing sequence. So $\{\alpha_n\}$ is a convergent sequence.
Passing to the limit, with the remark that $\alpha_n < 1/2$, we find that $\lim \alpha_n = 1/2$, and (2) implies that $f(x) \geq x/2 \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}^+$.
* Consequently, the answer to the problem is $\alpha = 1/2$.

title: è¶ٹهچ— ???? #39
problem_md:
Find the least value and the greatest value of the expression
$$
P = x + y
$$
where $x, y$ are real numbers satisfying the condition
$$
x - 3\sqrt{x + 1} = 3\sqrt{y + 2} - y.
$$
solution_md:
Write the given condition in the form
$$
x + y = 3(\sqrt{x+1} + \sqrt{y+2}).
$$
Denote by $G$ the set of values of $P$.
It is easily seen that:
$a \in G \Leftrightarrow a$ is a real number so that the following system of equations (with unknowns $x, y$) has solutions
$$
\begin{cases} 3(\sqrt{x+1} + \sqrt{y+2}) = a \\ x+y = a. \end{cases} \qquad (I)
$$
By putting $u = \sqrt{x+1}$ and $v = \sqrt{y+2}$, from the system (I), we get the following system of equations (with unknowns $u, v$)
$$
\begin{cases} 3(u+v) = a \\ u^2 + v^2 = a+3. \end{cases} \qquad (II)
$$
which is equivalent to
$$
\begin{cases} u+v = \frac{a}{3} \\ uv = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{a^2}{9} - a - 3 \right). \end{cases}
$$
Therefore,
The system (I) has solutions $\Leftrightarrow$ the system (II) has the solutions $(u, v)$ with $u, v$ non negative $\Leftrightarrow$ the equation (with unknown $t$) $18t^2 - 6at + a^2 - 9a - 27 = 0$ has two non negative roots $\Leftrightarrow$
$$
\begin{cases} -a^2 + 18a + 54 \ge 0 \\ a \ge 0 \\ a^2 - 9a - 27 \ge 0 \end{cases} \Leftrightarrow \frac{9 + 3\sqrt{21}}{2} \le a \le 9 + 3\sqrt{15}.
$$
Hence $G = \left[ \frac{9 + 3\sqrt{21}}{2} ; 9 + 3\sqrt{15} \right]$ and so $\min P = \frac{9 + 3\sqrt{21}}{2}$, $\max P = 9 + 3\sqrt{15}$.

title: è¶ٹهچ— ???? #40
problem_md:
In plane, let be given a fixed circle $(O)$ with radius $R$, two fixed points $A$, $B$ on the circle $(O)$ such that $A$, $B$, $O$ are not collinear.
Let $C$ be a point on $(O)$ distinct from $A$ and $B$. Construct the circle $(O_1)$ passing through $A$, touching the line $BC$ at $C$, construct the circle $(O_2)$ passing through $B$, touching the line $AC$ at $C$. The circle $(O_1)$ and $(O_2)$ intersect again at $D$ distinct from $C$.
Prove that
$$
i) \, CD \le R;$$
$$ii) \text{The line } CD \text{ passes through a fixed point when } C \text{ moves on } (O) \text{ so that } C \text{ does not coincide with } A \text{ and } B.$$ 
($(O)$ denotes a circle with center $O$.)
solution_md:
1) From the construction of the circles $(O_1)$ and $(O_2)$, we have:
$$
\angle CAD = \angle DCB \ (\text{subtend the arc } CD \text{ of } (O_1)) \quad (1)
$$
$$
\angle ACD = \angle CBD \ (\text{subtend the arc } CD \text{ of } (O_2)) \quad (2)
$$
Therefore $\triangle DAC \sim \triangle DCB$ and so
$$
\frac{DA}{DC} = \frac{DB}{DB}, \text{ i.e. } CD^2 = DA \cdot DB \quad (3)
$$
![](attached_image_1.png)
The law of cosines for triangle $ADB$ gives
$$
AB^2 = AD^2 + DB^2 - 2AD \cdot DB \cos(\angle ADB), \text{ so} \\
AB^2 \geq 2AD \cdot DB(1 - \cos(\angle ADB)) \quad (4)
$$
(3) and (4) imply that
$$
AB^2 \geq 2CD^2(1 - \cos(\angle ADB)) \quad (5)
$$
The law of sines for triangle $ABC$ gives $AB = 2R \sin(\angle ACB)$. So, from (5), we deduce that
$$
2R^2 \sin^2(\angle ACB) \geq CD^2(1 - \cos(\angle ADB)). \quad (6)
$$
From (1) and (2), we see that
$$
\begin{aligned} \angle CAD + \angle ACD &= \angle DCB + \angle CBD = \angle CAD + \angle CBD = \\
&= \angle DCB + \angle DCA = \angle ACB \end{aligned} \quad (7)
$$
Therefore
$$
\angle ADC = \angle BDC = 180^\circ - \angle ACB \quad (8)
$$
Moreover, it is easily seen that:
- $C$, $D$ lie on the same side with respect to the line $AB$
$$
\Leftrightarrow \angle CAD + \angle CBD < \angle CAB + \angle CBA;
$$
- $C$, $D$ lie on distinct sides with respect to the line $AB$
$$
\Leftrightarrow \angle CAD + \angle CBD > \angle CAB + \angle CBA.
$$
So, from (7), we deduce that:
- If $\angle ACB$ is acute then $C$, $D$ lie on the same side with respect to the line $AB$;
- If $\angle ACB$ is obtuse then $C$, $D$ lie on distinct sides with respect to the line $AB$.
Therefore, from (8),
when $\angle ACB$ is acute,
$$
\angle ADB = 360^\circ - (\angle ADC + \angle CDB) = 2\angle ACB
$$
when $\angle ACB$ is obtuse,
$$
\angle ADB = \angle ADC + \angle CDB = 360^\circ - 2\angle ACB.
$$
So, in all cases, $\cos(\angle ADB) = \cos(2\angle ACB)$.
Hence, from (6), we have $CD \le R$,
QED.

2) From the preceding results, it is easily seen that:
$$
- CD \text{ is the angle bisector of } \angle ADB; \qquad (9)
$$
$$
- O, D \text{ lie on the same side with respect to the line AB;} \qquad (10)
$$
$$
- \angle ADB = \angle AOB. \qquad (11)
$$
From (10), (11) we deduce that: when $C$ moves on $(O)$ (but $C$ distinct from $A, B$), the point $D$ moves on the arc $AOB$ of the fixed circle $(AOB)$. Therefore, (9) proves that the line $CD$ passes through a fixed point, namely the middle point $M$ of the arc $AB$ not containing $O$ of the circle $(AOB)$.

title: è¶ٹهچ— ???? #41
problem_md:
In plane, let be given an octagon $A_1A_2A_3A_4A_5A_6A_7A_8$ such that no three of its diagonals are concurrent. Each point of intersection of every two diagonals of the octagon is called a cross.
Consider the convex quadrilaterals whose vertices are vertices of the given octagon. Each such quadrilateral is called a subquadrilateral.
Find the least positive integer $n$ such that one can colour $n$ crosses so that for every $i, k \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8\}$ and $i \ne k$, if we denote by $s(i, k)$ the number of

subquadrilaterals admitting $A_i, A_k$ as vertices and admitting a coloured cross as the point of intersection of the diagonals then the values $s(i, k)$ are all equal.
solution_md:
Suppose that there are $n$ crosses which can be coloured so that the numbers $s(i, k)$ are all equal. Denote by $s$ the common value of these numbers.
Since for each couple of vertices $A_i, A_k$ of the octagon, there exist exactly $s$ subquadrilaterals admitting $A_i, A_k$ as vertices and admitting a coloured cross as the point of intersection of diagonals, hence the number of coloured crosses (with iterations) is $sC_8^2$.
Since for each coloured cross, there exist exactly $C_4^2$ couples of vertices of the octagon which define a same subquadrilateral admitting this coloured cross as the point of intersection of the diagonals, each coloured cross is counted $C_4^2$ times in the above mentioned numeration. Therefore,
$$
n = (s \cdot C_8^2) : C_4^2 = \frac{14s}{3}.
$$
As $n$ is a positive integer, $s$ is divisible by $3$. So $s \ge 3$ and it implies $n \ge 14$.
Now we describe a colouring of $14$ crosses so that numbers $s(i, k)$ are all equal. For this purpose, we shall denote a cross by a subquadrilateral admitting it as the point
of intersection of its diagonals and we shall denote the subquadrilateral $A_i A_k A_p A_q$ by $(ikpq)$. We colour the following crosses:
(1234), (1256), (1278), (1357), (1368), (1458), (1467);
(2358), (2367), (2457), (2468), (3456), (3478), (5678).
By direct verification for each couple of vertices of the octagon, we see that $s(i, k) = 3$ for all $i, k \in \{1, 2, 3, \dots, 8\}, i \neq k$.
So the required least positive integer $n$ is $n_{\min} = 14$.

title: è¶ٹهچ— ???? #42
problem_md:
Find all functions $f$ defined on $\mathbb{R}$, taking values in $\mathbb{R}$ so that
$$
f(f(x - y)) = f(x)f(y) - f(x) + f(y) - xy
$$
for all real numbers $x, y$.
solution_md:
Suppose that $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ satisfies the relation in the problem, i.e.
$$
f(f(x - y)) = f(x)f(y) - f(x) + f(y) - xy, \quad (1)
$$
for all $x, y \in \mathbb{R}$.
Put $f(0) = a$
By substituting $x = y = 0$ into (1) we get
$$
f(a) = a^2 \quad (2)
$$
By substituting $x = y = 0$ into (1), from (2) we get
$$
(f(x))^2 = x^2 + a^2, \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R} \quad (3)
$$
Therefore $(f(x))^2 = (f(-x))^2 \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}$, i.e.
$$
(f(x) + f(-x))(f(x) - f(-x)) = 0, \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R} \quad (4)
$$
Suppose that there exist $x_0 \neq 0$ such that $f(x_0) = f(-x_0)$.
By substituting $y = 0$ into (1), we get
$$
f(f(x)) = af(x) - f(x) + a, \forall x \in \mathbb{R} \quad (5)
$$
and by substituting $x = 0, y = -x$ into (1), we get
$$
f(f(x)) = af(-x) + f(-x) - a, \forall x \in \mathbb{R}. \quad (6)
$$
(5) and (6) give
$$
a(f(x) - f(-x) + f(x) + f(-x) = 2a, \forall x \in \mathbb{R}. \quad (7)
$$
By substituting $x = x_0$ into (7), we get
$$
f(x_0) = a \quad (*)
$$
On the other hand, from (3), we deduce that if $f(x_1) = f(x_2)$ then $x_1^2 = x_2^2$. Therefore, (*) implies that $x_0 = 0$ which contradicts the supposition that $x_0 \neq 0$. This contradiction proves that $f(x) \neq f(-x)$, $\forall x \in \mathbb{R}$. Therefore (4) shows that
$$
f(x) = f(-x) \quad \forall x \neq 0 \quad (8)
$$
By substituting (8) into (7), we get
$$
a(f(x) - 1) = 0 \quad \forall x \neq 0,
$$
and we deduce from it that $a = 0$, since if $a \neq 0$ then $f(x) = 1, \forall x \neq 0$ which contradicts (8).
So, from (3), we have
$$
(f(x))^2 = x^2 \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R} \quad (9)
$$
Now suppose that there exists $x_0 \neq 0$ such that $f(x_0) = x_0$. Then (5) implies that
$$
x_0 = f(x_0) = -f(f(x_0)) = -f(x_0) = -x_0.
$$
This contradiction proves that $f(x) \neq x, \forall x \neq 0$.
Hence (9) shows that $f(x) = -x, \forall x \in \mathbb{R}$.
By direct verification, it is easily seen that this function satisfies the condition of the problem.

So the function $f(x) = -x, \forall x \in \mathbb{R}$, is the unique function satisfying the conditions of the problem.

title: è¶ٹهچ— ???? #43
problem_md:
Find all triples of natural numbers $(x, y, n)$ satisfying the relation
$$
\frac{x! + y!}{n!} = 3^n
$$
(with the convention $0! = 1$).
solution_md:
The relation in the problem can be written in the form
$$
x! + y! = 3^n n! \quad (1)
$$
Suppose that $(x, y, n)$ is a triple of natural numbers satisfying (1).
It is easily seen that $n \ge 1$ and w.l.g. we can suppose that $x \le y$. We must now consider the following cases.

1) 1st case: $x \le n$
$$
\text{It is clear that } (1) \Leftrightarrow 1 + \frac{y!}{x!} = 3^n \frac{n!}{x!}. \qquad (2)
$$
(2) implies that $1 + \frac{y!}{x!} \equiv 0 \pmod{3}$. Since the product of three consecutive integers is divisible by 3 and since $n \ge 1$, we have: $x < y \le x + 2$.

a) If $y = x + 2$, (2) implies
$$
1 + (x + 1)(x + 2) = 3^n \frac{n!}{x!} \qquad (3)
$$
Since the product of two consecutive integers is divisible by 2, from (3), we deduce that $n \le x + 1$.
- If $n = x$, (3) implies
$$
1 + (x + 1)(x + 2) = 3^x, \quad \text{i.e. } x^2 + 3x + 3 = 3^x \qquad (4)
$$
Since $x \ge 1$, (4) shows that $x \equiv 0 \pmod{3}$, therefore $x \ge 3$ and we get a contradiction
$$
3 = x^2 + 3x - 3^x \equiv 0 \pmod{9}
$$
which proves that $n \ne x$.
- If $n = x + 1$, (3) implies that
$$
1 + (x + 1)(x + 2) = 3^n(x + 1)
$$
hence $x + 1$ is a positive divisor of 1. Therefore $x = 0$ and consequently $y = 2, n = 1$.

b) If $y = x + 1$, (2) implies
$$
x + 2 = 3^n \frac{n!}{x!} \qquad (5)
$$
Since $x \ge 1$, (5) shows that $x \ge 1$ and then $x = n$ and
$$
x + 2 = 3^x. \qquad (6)
$$
$x = 1$ is the unique natural number satisfying (6) therefore, in this case, if the triples $(x, y, n)$ satisfy (1) then $(x, y, n) = (0, 2, 1)$ or $(x, y, n) = (1, 2, 1)$.

2) 2nd case: $x > n$.
It is clear that
$$
(1) \Leftrightarrow \frac{x!}{n!} + \frac{y!}{n!} = 3^n. \qquad (7)
$$
Since $n + 1$ and $n + 2$ can not be simultaneously the powers of 3, from (7), we deduce that $x = n + 1$. Then (2) implies that
$$
n + 1 + \frac{y!}{n!} = 3^n. \qquad (8)
$$
Since $y \ge x$, we see that $y \ge n + 1$. By putting $A = \frac{y!}{(n+1)!}$, we can write (8) in the form
$$
(n + 1)(1 + A) = 3^n. \qquad (9)
$$
It is clear that if $y \ge n + 4$ then $A \equiv 0 \mod 3$, and $A + 1$ can not be a power of 3. Hence (9) shows that $y \le n + 3$.
So $n + 1 \le y \le n + 3$.

a) If $y = n + 3$ then $A = (n + 2)(n + 3)$, and from (9) we get
$$
(n + 1)(1 + (n + 2)(n + 3)) = 3^n \text{ i.e. } (n + 2)^3 - 1 = 3^n. \qquad (10)
$$
It follows that $n > 2$ and $n + 2 = 1 \mod 3$. By putting $n + 2 = 3k + 1, k > 2$, we can write (10) in the form
$$
9k(3k^2 + 3k + 1) = 3^{3k-1}.
$$
It implies that $3k^2 + 3k + 1$ is a power of 3. This contradiction proves that $y \ne n + 3$.

b) If $y = n + 2$ then $A = n + 2$ and from (9) we get
$$
(n + 1)(n + 3) = 3^n. \qquad (11)
$$

c) If $y = n + 1$ then $A = 1$ and from (9) we get
$$
2(n + 1) = 3^n.
$$
It is clear that there exist no $n$ satisfying this relation. So $y \ne n + 1$.

Thus, if the triple $(x, y, n)$, with $x \le y$, satisfies (1) then $n \ge x$.
Consequently, if $(x, y, n)$ is a triple of natural numbers satisfying (1) then $(x, y, n) = (0, 2, 1)$ or $(x, y, n) = (2, 0, 1)$, or $(x, y, n) = (2, 1, 0)$, or $(x, y, n) = (2, 1, 1)$.
By direct verification, we see that the four mentioned triples satisfy (1). So these triples are all triples satisfying the conditions of the problem.

title: è¶ٹهچ— 2006 #44
problem_md:
Solve the following system of equations
$$
\begin{cases}
\sqrt{x^2 - 2x + 6} \log_3 (6-y) = x \\
\sqrt{y^2 - 2y + 6} \log_3 (6-z) = y \\
\sqrt{z^2 - 2z + 6} \log_3 (6-x) = z.
\end{cases}
$$
solution_md:
Conditions for $x$, $y$, $z$ of the definition of the expressions in the equation are: $x, y, z < 6$.
The given system of equations is equivalent to the system:
$$
\left\{
\begin{aligned}
\log_3(6-y) &= \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2-2x+6}} && (1) \\
\log_3(6-z) &= \frac{y}{\sqrt{y^2-2y+6}} && (2) \\
\log_3(6-x) &= \frac{z}{\sqrt{z^2-2z+6}} && (3)
\end{aligned}
\right.
$$
The function $f(x) = \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2-2x+6}}$ is increasing since
$$
f'(x) = \frac{6-x}{(x^2 - 2x + 6)\sqrt{x^2 - 2x + 6}} > 0
$$
for $x < 6$.
The function $g(x) = \log_3(6-x)$ is decreasing for $x < 6$.
We now prove that if $(x, y, z)$ is a solution to the given system of equations then $x = y = z = 3$.
Without loss of generality, suppose that $\max(x, y, z) = x$. We must consider two cases:

i) $x \geq y \geq z$

As $f(x) = \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2-2x+6}}$ is increasing, (1), (2), (3) show that
$$
\log_3(6-y) \geq \log_3(6-z) \geq \log_3(6-x),
$$
so $x \ge z \ge y$. But $y \ge z$, so $z = y$. Then (1) and (2) imply that $x = y = z$.

ii) $x \geq z \geq y$

Analogously, we have $\log_3(6-y) \ge \log_3(6-x) \ge \log_3(6-z)$ and so $z \ge x \ge y$. But $x \ge z$, so $x = z$. Then (1) and (3) imply that $x = y = z$.

The equation $f(x) = g(x)$ has a unique root $x = 3$. Consequently, the given system of equations has a unique solution $x = y = z = 3$.

title: è¶ٹهچ— 2006 #45
problem_md:
Let be given a convex quadrilateral **ABCD**. A point **M** moves on the line **AB** but does not coincide with **A** and **B**. Let **N** be the second point of meeting (distinct from **M**) of the circles (**MAC**) and (**MBD**). Prove that

i) $N$ moves on a fixed circle,

ii) The line $MN$ passes through a fixed point.

(The symbol $(XYZ)$ denotes the circle passing through the points $X$, $Y$, $Z$.)
solution_md:
Let $I$ be the point of intersection of the two diagonals of the quadrilateral $ABCD$ (figure 1).

i) The quadrilateral $DCIN$ is cyclic because $\widehat{ICN} = \widehat{IDN}$ (as both angles are equal to $\widehat{AMN}$). Consequently, $N$ moves on the fixed circle.

ii) Draw the line $l$ passing through $I$ parallel to $AB$. It cuts $MN$ at $K$. The quadrilateral $IKCN$ is cyclic because $\widehat{ICN} = \widehat{IKN}$ (as both angles are equal to $\widehat{AMN}$). Therefore, the second meeting point of $l$ with the circle $(IDC)$ passes through a fixed point.

title: è¶ٹهچ— 2006 #46
problem_md:
Let $m$ and $n$ be two integers greater than $3$ and let be given a rectangular $m \times n$ board. At each step, one puts simultaneously $4$ marbles into $4$ cells of the board (each marble into a cell) so that these four cells form one of the following schemata.
![](attached_image_1.png)
![](attached_image_2.png)
![](attached_image_3.png)
![](attached_image_4.png)
Is it true that starting from a rectangular $m \times n$ board without marbles in it after a finite number of steps of appropriate puttings, one can put marbles into all cells of the board so that each cell is filled with a same (positive) number of marbles for all cells when:
i) $m = 2004$ and $n = 2006$?
ii) $m = 2005$ and $n = 2006$?
(At each step, it is not necessary that the four cells which are selected to put marbles into contained no marbles).
solution_md:
i) After two steps: one can put into each cell of a small board of size $(4 \times 2)$ a marble. One can partition the given board of size $(2004 \times 2006)$ into small boards of size $(4 \times 2)$. Therefore, after some steps, one can put marbles into all cells of the given board so that the number of marbles in each cell is the same for all cells.

ii) We now prove by contradiction that in the second case, the response to the problem is "no". Indeed, suppose that the contrary: after some steps, there would be $k$ marbles ($k > 0$) in each cell of the given board of size $(2005 \times 2006)$. Color black all cells belonging to the odd rows and consider each non colored cell as white. Then, the number of black cells is equal to $1003 \times 2006$ and the number of white cells is equal to $1002 \times 2006$. But at each step we put exactly $2$ marbles into black cells and $2$ marbles in white cells. Therefore, after an arbitrary number of steps, the number of marbles in all black cells must be equal to the number of the marbles in all white cells. Consequently, we would have $1003 \times 2006 \times k = 1002 \times 2006 \times k$ and so get $1 = 0$. This contradiction proves our assertion.

title: è¶ٹهچ— 2006 #47
problem_md:
Consider the function
$$
f(x) = -x + \sqrt{(x + a)(x + b)}
$$
where $a$ and $b$ are two given distinct positive real numbers.
Prove that for every real number $s$ belonging to the interval $(0,1)$ there exists a unique real number $\alpha$ such that
$$
f(\alpha) = \left( \frac{a^s + b^s}{2} \right)^{\frac{1}{s}}
$$
solution_md:
It is easily seen that $f(x)$ is a continuous function on $[0, \infty)$. We shall prove the following assertions:

i) $f(x)$ is strictly increasing on $[0, \infty)$;

ii) $f(0) = \sqrt{ab}$, $\lim_{x \to \infty} f(x) = \frac{a+b}{2}$;

iii) for every $s$ with $0 < s < 1$, we have:
$$
\sqrt{ab} < \left(\frac{a^s + b^s}{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{s}} \le \frac{a+b}{2}.
$$
With these assertions, the intermediate value theorem of continuous functions proves the existence of a unique $\alpha$ such that $f(\alpha) = \left(\frac{a^s + b^s}{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{s}}$.

Proof of i):
$$
f'(x) = -1 + \frac{2x+a+b}{2\sqrt{(a+x)(b+x)}} = \frac{(\sqrt{a+x}-\sqrt{b+x})^2}{2\sqrt{(a+x)(b+x)}} > 0.
$$

Proof of ii):
It is evident that $f(0) = \sqrt{ab}$ and:
$$
\lim_{x \to \infty} f(x) = \lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{-x^2 + (a+x)(b+x)}{x + \sqrt{(a+x)(b+x)}} = \lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{a+b+\frac{ab}{x}}{1+\sqrt{\left(\frac{a}{x}+1\right)\left(\frac{b}{x}+1\right)}} = \frac{a+b}{2}.
$$

Proof of iii):
The first inequality is deduced from the A-G-mean inequality.
Put $m = \left(\frac{a^s + b^s}{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{s}}$, $x = \frac{a}{m}$, $y = \frac{b}{m}$. Clearly, $x^s + y^s = 2$ and Bernoulli inequality implies that:
$$
x = (1 + x^s - 1)^{\frac{1}{s}} \ge 1 + \frac{x^s - 1}{s}
$$
$$
y = (1 + y^s - 1)^{\frac{1}{s}} \ge 1 + \frac{y^s - 1}{s}
$$
(both equalities do not hold simultaneously). From these inequalities, by adding both left sides together and both right sides together, we get $x + y > 2$.

title: è¶ٹهچ— 2006 #48
problem_md:
Find all polynomials $P(x)$ with real coefficients satisfying the condition
$$
P(x^2) + x(3P(x) + P(-x)) = (P(x))^2 + 2x^2
$$
for all real numbers $x$.
solution_md:
Let $P(x)$ be a polynomial satisfying the condition of the problem. We have:
$$
P(x^2) + x(3P(x) + P(-x)) = (P(x))^2 + 2x^2, \quad x \in \mathbb{R}, \quad (1)
$$

It is clear that $\deg P > 0$. Consider the cases:
i) $\deg P = 1$.
$P(x)$ has the form $P(x) = ax + b$, $a \neq 0$. Substituting it into (1) gives:
$$
(a^2 - 3a + 2)x^2 + 2b(a - 2b)x + b^2 - b \equiv 0 \quad (2)
$$
It is evident that:
$$
(2) \Leftrightarrow (a = 1, b = 0), (a = 2, b = 0), (a = 2, b = 1).
$$
So we get the polynomials: $P(x) = x$, $P(x) = 2x$, and $P(x) = 2x + 1$.

Put $P(x) = ax^n + S(x)$, $a \neq 0$, (3).
Where $S(x)$ is a polynomial of degree $\deg S(x) = k < n$.
Substituting (3) into (1) gives:
$$
(a^2 - a)x^{2n} + (S(x))^2 - S(x^2) + 2ax^n \cdot S(x) \equiv \\
\equiv (3 + (-1)^n)ax^{n+1} + (3S(x) + S(-x))x - 2x^2. \quad (4)
$$
Since the degree of left hand side of (4) is equal to $(n + 1)$ and since $n + 1 < 2n$, (4) implies that $a^2 - a = 0$ and so $a = 1$. Therefore, from (4) we obtain:
$$
2x^n S(x) + (S(x))^2 - S(x^2) \equiv (3 + (-1)^n)x^{n+1} + (3S(x) + S(-x))x - 2x^2 \quad (5)
$$

Since degree of the left hand side of (5) is equal to $(n + k)$ and the degree of right hand side of (5) is equal to $(n+1)$, (5) implies that $k = 1$.
Moreover, by substituting $x = 0$ into (5), we obtain $(S(0))^2 - S(0) = 0$, i.e $S(0) = 0$ or $S(0) = 1$. So $S(x) = px$ or $S(x) = px + 1$.

a) In case $S(x) = px$, by substituting it into (5), we get:
$$
(3 + (-1)^n - 2p)x^{n+1} - (p^2 - 3p + 2)x^2 \equiv 0
$$
Which is equivalent to:
$$
\begin{cases} 3 + (-1)^n - 2p = 0 \\ p^2 - 3p + 2 = 0 \end{cases}
$$
$$
\Leftrightarrow p = 1, n \equiv 1 \pmod{2}
$$
$$
\text{or } p = 2, n \equiv 0 \pmod{2}.
$$
So we obtain the polynomials:
$$
P(x) = x^{2n+1} + x \text{ and } P(x) = x^{2n} + 2x.
$$
Direct verifications shows that these polynomials satisfy (1).

b) In case $S(x) = px + 1$, by substituting it in to (5) we get:
$$
(3 + (-1)^n - 2p)x^{n+1} - 2x^n - (p^2 - 3p + 2)x^2 - 2(p-2)x \equiv 0
$$
which implies that $2 \equiv 0$. This contradiction show that in this case, there is no $S(x)$ satisfying (5) and so there is no $P(x)$ satisfying (1).

All in all, the polynomials $P(x)$ satisfying the condition of the problem are:
$$
P(x) = x, \quad P(x) = x^{2n} + 2x, \quad n \in \mathbb{N}
$$
$$
P(x) = x^{2n+1} + x, \quad n \in \mathbb{N}.
$$

title: è¶ٹهچ— 2006 #49
problem_md:
Consider a set $S$ of 2006 distinct numbers. A subset $T$ of $S$ is called stubborn if for every $u, v$ (not necessarily distinct) in $T$, the number $u + v$ does not belong to $T$. Prove that

i) if $S$ is the set of 2006 first positive integers, then the number of elements of every stubborn subset $T$ of $S$ does not exceed 1003,

ii) if $S$ consists of 2006 arbitrary positive integers then there exists a stubborn subset $T$ of $S$ having 669 elements.
solution_md:
i) Let $A$ be a stubborn subset of $S = \{1, 2, \dots, 2006\}$ consisting of $x$ elements $a_1 < a_2 < \dots < a_x$. Consider the set $B := \{a_2 - a_1, a_3 - a_1, \dots, a_x - a_1\}$. It is a subset of $S$ and consists of $x-1$ elements. As $A$ is a stubborn subset of $S$, $A \cap B = \emptyset$. It implies that $x + (x-1) \leq 2006$, so $x \leq 1003$.


ii) Let $S = \{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_{2006}\}$. Consider the product $P$ of all odd divisors of $\prod_{i=1}^{2006} a_i$. It is easy to see that there exists a prime number $p$ of the form $p = 3r+2$ such that $p$ is a divisor of $3P+2$; $p$ is coprime with every $a_i$ ($i = 1, 2, \dots, 2006$). For each $a \in S$, the sequence $a, 2a, \dots, (p-1)a$ (mod $p$) is a permutation of $1, 2, \dots, p-1$, therefore there exists a set $S_a$ consisting of $r+1$ integers $x$ in $\overline{1, p-1}$ so that $xa\ (\text{mod}\ p)$ belongs to $A = \{r+1, \dots, 2r+1\}$. For each $x \in \overline{1, p-1}$, let $S_x = \{a \in S \mid xa \in A\}$. We have:
$$
|S_1| + |S_2| + \dots + |S_{p-1}| = \sum_{a \in S} |A_a| = 2006 \times (r+1)
$$
So there exists $x_0$ such that $|S_{x_0}| \geq \frac{2006 \times (r+1)}{3r+1} > 668$. Let $B$ be a subset consisting of 669 elements of $S_{x_0}$ then $B$ is a stubborn subset of $S$. Indeed, if $u, v, w \in B$ ($u$ can be equal to $v$) then $x_0u, x_0v, x_0w \in A$. It is easy to verify that $x_0u + x_0v \neq x_0w\ (\text{mod}\ p)$ therefore $u + v \neq w$.

title: è¶ٹهچ— 2007 #50
problem_md:
Solve the equation system
$$
\begin{cases} 1 - \frac{12}{y+3x} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{x}} \\ 1 + \frac{12}{y+3x} = \frac{6}{\sqrt{x}} \end{cases}
$$
solution_md:
The necessary conditions for the given equation system are $x, y > 0; y+3x \neq 0$. The given equation system is equivalent to the following equation systems
$$
\begin{cases} 1 - \frac{12}{y+3x} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{x}} \\ 1 + \frac{12}{y+3x} = \frac{6}{\sqrt{x}} \end{cases} \Rightarrow \begin{cases} \frac{1}{\sqrt{x}} + \frac{1}{\sqrt{y}} = 1 \\ -\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}} + \frac{3}{\sqrt{y}} = \frac{12}{y+3x} \end{cases} \quad (1) \quad (2)
$$
Multiply (1) with (2), we get
$$
\frac{9}{y} - \frac{1}{x} = \frac{12}{y+3x} \Leftrightarrow y^2 + 6xy - 27x^2 = 0 \Leftrightarrow \begin{cases} y = 3x \\ y = -9x \end{cases}
$$
By the condition $x, y > 0$, we have $y = 3x$. By setting $y = 3x$ in (1), we obtain
$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}} + \frac{3}{\sqrt{3x}} = 1 \Rightarrow x = 4 + 2\sqrt{3}, y = 12 + 6\sqrt{3}
$$

title: è¶ٹهچ— 2007 #51
problem_md: Let $\triangle ABC$ be a triangle with two fixed vertices $B, C$ and the vertex $A$ is variable. Let $H$ and $G$ be the orthocenter and the centroid of the triangle $ABC$ respectively. Find the locus of $A$ such that the midpoint $K$ of the segment $HG$ moves on the line $BC$.
solution_md:
Choose orthogonal Cartesian coordinate system $Oxy$ where $O$ is the midpoint of the segment $BC$ and $Oy$ is the line $BC$. Denote by $2a > 0$ the length of the segment $BC$. The coordinates of the vertices $B$ and $C$ are $B(-a, 0)$ and $C(a, 0)$. Suppose that $A$ has the coordinates $A(x_0, y_0)$ ($y_0 \neq 0$), then the coordinates of the orthocenter $H$ satisfy the following equations
$$
\begin{cases}
x_H = x_0 \\
(x_H + a)(a - x_0) - y_0 y_H = 0
\end{cases}
$$
hence $H\left(x_0, \frac{a^2 - x_0^2}{y_0}\right)$. The centroid $G$ has the coordinates $\left(\frac{x_0}{3}, \frac{y_0}{3}\right)$. The midpoint $K$ of $HG$ has the coordinates $\left(\frac{2x_0}{3}, \frac{3a^2 - 3x_0^2 + y_0^2}{6y_0}\right)$. The point $K$ belongs to the line $BC$ if and only if
$$
3a^2 - 3x_0^2 + y_0^2 = 0 \iff \frac{x_0^2}{a^2} - \frac{y_0^2}{3a^2} = 1 \quad (y_0 \neq 0).
$$
Thus, the locus of $A$ is the hyperbola $\frac{x_0^2}{a^2} - \frac{y_0^2}{3a^2} = 1$ except the two points $B$ and $C$.

title: è¶ٹهچ— 2007 #52
problem_md: Given a regular 2007-polygon. Find the smallest positive integer $k$ satisfying the following property: In every set of $k$ vertices there are 4 vertices which form a quadrilateral with 3 edges of the given 2007-polygon.
solution_md:
Denote the vertices of the regular 2007-polygon by $A_1, A_2, \dots, A_{2007}$. Note that every quadrilateral has 3 edges of the given polygon if and only if its 4 vertices are consecutive vertices of the polygon.

Denote by $A$ the set of the vertices except $A_{4k}$ ($k=1, 2, \dots, 501$) and $A_{2007}$, in fact, $A = \{A_1, A_2, A_3, A_5, A_6, A_7, \dots, A_{2005}, A_{2006}\}$. Let $|A| = 1505$ and $A$ has no 4 consecutive vertices of the given 2007-polygon. Thus, $k \ge 1506$.

Now, we will prove that every subset $B$ of 1506 vertices contains 4 consecutive vertices of the given 2007-polygon. Let $T$ be a subset of arbitrary 1506 vertices of the given 2007-polygon. By deleting 501 vertices not belonging to $B$ we obtain a decomposition of the set of the vertices of the given 2007-polygon into the subsets $B_1, B_2, \dots, B_m$ with $m \le 501$. By the Dirichlet principle, there is a set $B_i$ containing $\ge \frac{1506}{501} > 3$ consecutive vertices.

title: è¶ٹهچ— 2007 #53
problem_md:
Let $b$ be a positive real number. Find all functions $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ satisfying
$$
f(x+y) = f(x) \cdot 3^{b^y} + f(y)^{-1} + b^x (3^{b^y} + f(y)^{-1} - b^y) \quad \forall x, y \in \mathbb{R}.
$$
solution_md:
The given equation system is equivalent to the following equation system
$$
f(x+y) + b^{x+y} = (f(x)+b^x)3^{b^x+f(y)-1} \quad \forall x,y \in \mathbb{R} \quad (1)
$$
Let $g(x) = f(x) + b^x$. Then $(1) \Leftrightarrow g(x+y) = g(x)3^{g(y)-1} \quad \forall x, y \in \mathbb{R} \quad (2)$

Substitute $y = 0$ in (2) we get
$$
g(x) = g(x)3^{g(0)-1} \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R} \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad \begin{cases} g(x) = 0 & \forall x \in \mathbb{R} \\ g(0) = 1 \end{cases}
$$
*) If $g(x) = 0$ $\forall x \in \mathbb{R}$ then $f(x) = -b^x$.

*) If $g(0)=1$, then by putting $x=0$ in (2) we get
$$
g(y) = g(0)3^{g(y-1)} \Leftrightarrow g(y) = 3^{g(y-1)} \Leftrightarrow 3^{g(y-1)} - g(y) = 0, \forall y \in \mathbb{R}. \quad (3)
$$
Consider the function $h(t) = 3^{t-1} - t$ we get $h'(t) = 3^{t-1} \ln 3 - 1$.
$$
h'(t) = 0 \iff t = \log_3(\log_3 e) + 1 < 1.
$$

| t | $-\infty$ | $\log_3(\log_3 e) + 1$ | $1$ | $+\infty$ |
|---|-----------|--------------------------|-----|-----------|
| $h'(t)$ | $-$ | $0$ | $+$ | $+$ |
| $h(t)$ | (0, $+\infty$) | | | |

From the table we easily see that the equation $h(t) = 0$ has two roots $t_1 = 1$ and $t_2 = c$ with $0 < c < 1$. Thus,
$$
g(y) = 3^{g(y)-1} \iff \begin{cases} g(y) = 1 \\ g(y) = c \quad c = \text{const}, 0 < c < 1 \end{cases} \quad \forall y \in \mathbb{R} \quad (4)
$$
Suppose that there exists $y_0 \in \mathbb{R}$ such that $g(y_0) = c$. Then
$$
1 = g(0) = g(y_0 - y_0) = g(-y_0) \cdot 3^{g(y_0)-1} = c \cdot g(-y_0).
$$
This implies that $g(-y_0) = \frac{1}{c} \neq c$, which contradicts (4). Therefore $g(y) = 1 \quad \forall y \in \mathbb{R}$, which implies that $f(x) = 1 - b^x$.

Thus, there are two functions $f(x) = -b^x$ and $f(x) = 1 - b^x$.

title: è¶ٹهچ— 2007 #54
problem_md: Let $ABCD$ be a trapezium with the bottom edge $BC$ ($BC \parallel AC$ and $BC > AD$) and inscribed in the circle $(O)$ ($O$ is the center of $(O)$). Let $P$ be a point moving on the line $BC$ outside the segment $BC$ such that $PA$ doesn't touch the circle $(O)$. The circle with the diameter $PD$ intersects $(O)$ in $E$ ($E \neq D$). Let $M$ be the point of intersection of $BC$ and $DE$, and $N$ ($N \neq A$) be the second point of intersection of $PA$ and $(O)$. Prove that the line $MN$ passes through a fixed point.
solution_md:
Let $A'$ be the image of the point $A$ by the reflection through the point $O$. We prove that $N$, $M$, $A'$ are collinear and therefore the line $MN$ passes the fixed point $A'$. First, $DE$ is the radical axis of the circle $(O)$ and the circle ($\gamma_1$) with the diameter $PD$.

Since $\angle PNA' = 90^\circ$ the line $NA'$ is the radical axis of the circle $(O)$ and the circle ($\gamma_2$) with the diameter $PA'$.

The line $DA'$ meets the line $BC$ at the point $F$; since $\angle PFA' = 90^\circ$, $\angle ADA' = 90^\circ$ $\Rightarrow \angle PFA' = 90^\circ$, therefore $BC$ is the axis of the circle ($\gamma_1$) and ($\gamma_2$), thus the radical axis $DE$, $BC$ and $NA'$ are concurrent at the radical center $M$, thus the points $M$, $N$ and $A'$ are collinear.

title: è¶ٹهچ— 2007 #55
problem_md: Let $a > 2$ be a real number and $f_n(x) = a^{10} x^{n+10} + x^n + \dots + x + 1$ ($n = 1, 2, \dots$). Prove that for every positive integer $n$ the equation $f_n(x) = a$ has exactly a real root $x_n \in (0; +\infty)$. Prove that the sequence $(x_n)$ has a finite limit when $n \to +\infty$.
solution_md:
For every $n$ we define $g_n(x) = f_n(x) - a$. Then $g_n(x)$ is a continuous and increasing function on $[0; +\infty)$. We have $g_n(0) = 1 - a < 0$; $g_n(1) = a^{10} + n + 1 - a > 0$ so $g_n(x) = 0$ has the only root $x_n$ in $(0; +\infty)$.

To prove the existence of the limit $\lim_{n \to \infty} x_n$, we prove that the sequence $(x_n)$, $n=1, 2, \dots$, is increasing and confined.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{We have } & g_n\left(1-\frac{1}{a}\right) = a^{10}\left(1-\frac{1}{a}\right)^{n+10} + \frac{1-\left(1-\frac{1}{a}\right)^{n+1}}{1} - a \\
& = a\left(1-\frac{1}{a}\right)^{n+1}\left(a^9\left(1-\frac{1}{a}\right)^9 - 1\right) = a\left(1-\frac{1}{a}\right)^{n+1}\left((a-1)^9 - 1\right) > 0.
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{Thus } x_n < 1 - \frac{1}{a} \quad \forall n.
$$

On the other hand $g_n(x_n) = a^{10}x_n^{n+10} + x_n^n + \dots + 1 - a = 0$, therefore

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_n g_n(x_n) &= a^{10} x_n^{n+11} + x_n^{n+1} + \dots + x_n - a x_n = 0 \\
\Rightarrow g_{n+1}(x_n) &= x_n g_n(x_n) + 1 + a x_n - a = a x_n + 1 - a < 0 \text{ for } x_n < 1 - \frac{1}{a}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the function $g_{n+1}$ is increasing and $0 = g_{n+1}(x_{n+1}) > g_{n+1}(x_n)$ then we have $x_n < x_{n+1}$. Thus the sequence $(x_n)$, $n=1, 2, \dots$, is increasing and confined, and therefore there exists $\lim_{n \to \infty} x_n$.

title: è¶ٹهچ— 2008 #57
problem_md:
Given an angle $xOy$ on a plane. Let $M$ and $N$ are variable points on the rays $Ox$ and $Oy$, respectively. 2/ Find all values of $k$ such that the three circles $(O_1)$, $(O_2)$ and $(O_3)$ have exactly two common points.
